title: Steady Hands, Watchful Eyes
overview: The US-Iran standoff over the Strait of Hormuz continues to dominate headlines, with knock-on effects for Turkish monetary policy and the lira rather than any direct security threat to residents here. Domestically, migration enforcement and a new opposition party launch are the stories worth tracking.

sections.heading: Regional Tensions: Watch, Don't Worry
sections.body: Washington has warned it will strike Iranian infrastructure in response to any attack on shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, and Tehran has promised a 'powerful' reply to any hit on its own territory. Turkey is not a party to this exchange, and Iran's foreign minister publicly credited Ankara with helping prevent a separate Kurdish-group offensive from Iraqi territory during the earlier phase of the conflict. The US travel advisory for Türkiye remains unchanged at Level 2 — exercise increased caution, nothing more. Iran, Iraq, Syria and Lebanon sit at Level 4; if you're transiting through the region, check current guidance before booking.

sections.heading: Lira and Your Wallet
sections.body: The Central Bank is expected to hold rates steady again, with the Gulf conflict complicating the disinflation path Bloomberg and others had been tracking. USD/TRY sits at 47.2, EUR/TRY at 53.84. Analysts at Commerzbank and elsewhere flag rising inflation expectations as the near-term risk to watch; Citigroup has already trimmed its Turkish bond exposure given oil price moves. On a lighter note, Turkish credit card spending abroad hit a record 93.1 billion lira in May — a reminder that outbound travel and cross-border shopping remain strong despite the exchange rate.

sections.heading: Migration Enforcement Continues
sections.body: Authorities conducted nationwide raids resulting in 355 detentions this week, part of ongoing enforcement rather than a new policy shift. If your ikamet or work permit paperwork is current, this shouldn't affect you directly, but it's a useful nudge to keep documents up to date and accessible.

sections.heading: Politics: A New Party, an Old Question
sections.body: Former CHP figure Özgür Özel has launched a new party positioning itself against the current government — one more data point in Turkey's shifting opposition landscape, worth noting but not yet consequential for daily administration or services.

closing: Nothing here requires you to change your plans today — just keep half an eye on the news and your paperwork in order.
